---
title: "Encrypted Mempool"
description: "Build, sign, and submit encrypted-mempool transactions with the Aptos TypeScript SDK."
sidebar:
  label: "Encrypted Mempool"
---

import { Aside, Steps } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

Encrypted-mempool transactions wrap your Move payload in a ciphertext at build time, so it stays encrypted while pending and is decrypted by validators just before execution. The flow is the same as a plaintext transaction — **build → sign → submit → wait** — with a few extra `options` passed to `aptos.transaction.build.*`.

<Aside type="note">
  **Encrypted mempool** is the protocol name ([AIP-144](https://github.com/aptos-foundation/AIPs/blob/main/aips/aip-144-encrypted-mempool.md)); some product material calls it **encrypted pending transactions** — same feature. See the [Encrypted Pending Transactions](/build/guides/encrypted-pending-transactions) guide for background on visibility.
</Aside>

## Prerequisites

Encrypted mempool is currently live on **devnet** and **testnet**, with **mainnet** to follow. The network you submit to must support the feature; check the ledger info first:

```typescript
const ledgerInfo = await aptos.getLedgerInfo();
const supportsEncrypted = Boolean(ledgerInfo.encryption_key);
```

If the node doesn't expose `encryption_key`, building with `encrypted: true` throws.

The `senderAuthenticationKey` option is **optional** — the SDK fetches it from chain and caches it for ~1 hour per address. Pass it explicitly to bypass the lookup, which is useful immediately after a key rotation. It accepts an `AuthenticationKey` instance or a raw 32-byte hex string / `Uint8Array` — call `publicKey.authKey()` to derive an `AuthenticationKey` from a public key. For accounts that have never rotated their key, the account address bytes are identical to the authentication key bytes, so you can pass the address hex directly.

## Build, sign, and submit

<Steps>
  1. Build with `encrypted: true`.

     ```typescript filename="encrypted-transfer.ts"
     const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
       sender: sender.accountAddress,
       data: {
         function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
         functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
       },
       options: {
         encrypted: true,
         // senderAuthenticationKey is optional — SDK fetches from chain automatically
       },
     });
     ```

  2. Sign and submit as usual.

     ```typescript filename="encrypted-transfer.ts"
     const pending = await aptos.signAndSubmitTransaction({
       signer: sender,
       transaction,
     });

     await aptos.waitForTransaction({ transactionHash: pending.hash });
     ```
</Steps>

<Aside type="note">
  Encrypted transactions pay a minimum gas-unit price of **200 Octas/gas-unit** (twice the network base minimum). The SDK enforces this floor at build time: prices below it are bumped automatically, and the SDK warns when a caller explicitly set a lower value.
</Aside>

## Simulating an encrypted transaction

`aptos.transaction.simulate.*` rejects encrypted payloads — simulation is not supported directly. It is possible to simulate indirectly using a plaintext build, but this is **strongly discouraged**: simulation is unencrypted, and submitting a simulation closely followed by an encrypted transaction with the same payload allows observers to correlate the two and infer the encrypted transaction's contents.

If you must simulate to estimate gas, build a **plaintext twin** with the same `data`, simulate that, then build again with `encrypted: true` for submission:

1. `build.simple({ ..., options: { /* no encrypted */ } })` — same function, arguments, gas hints.
2. `aptos.transaction.simulate.simple({ ... })` — tune `maxGasAmount` / `gasUnitPrice` from the result.
3. `build.simple({ ..., options: { encrypted: true, ...gas from step 2 } })` — submit this build.

## Multi-agent

`senderAuthenticationKey` and `secondarySignerAuthenticationKeys` are both optional — any omitted or `undefined` entry is fetched from chain. Pass them explicitly to skip the lookup (e.g. right after key rotation). Entries must be in the same order as `secondarySignerAddresses`.

```typescript filename="encrypted-multi-agent.ts"
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.multiAgent({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  secondarySignerAddresses: [secondary.accountAddress],
  data: {
    function: "0x<module>::<contract>::<multi_agent_entry>",
    functionArguments: [/* ... */],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    senderAuthenticationKey: sender.publicKey.authKey(),           // optional
    secondarySignerAuthenticationKeys: [secondary.publicKey.authKey()], // optional; entries can be undefined
  },
});
```

## Fee payer (sponsored)

`feePayerAuthenticationKey` is optional when `feePayerAddress` is a non-zero sponsor — omit it and the SDK fetches it from chain automatically.

```typescript filename="encrypted-sponsored.ts"
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  withFeePayer: true,
  data: {
    function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
    functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    senderAuthenticationKey: sender.publicKey.authKey(),   // optional
    feePayerAuthenticationKey: sponsor.publicKey.authKey(), // optional
  },
});
```

## Orderless

Pass `replayProtectionNonce` together with `encrypted: true`. The transaction uses orderless replay protection while the payload stays encrypted. See [Orderless Transactions](/build/sdks/ts-sdk/building-transactions/orderless-transactions).

```typescript filename="encrypted-orderless.ts"
const transaction = await aptos.transaction.build.simple({
  sender: sender.accountAddress,
  data: {
    function: "0x1::aptos_account::transfer",
    functionArguments: [receiver.accountAddress, amount],
  },
  options: {
    encrypted: true,
    replayProtectionNonce: 12345n,
    // senderAuthenticationKey is optional — SDK fetches from chain automatically
  },
});
```

## `claimedEntryFunction` (optional)

You can set `claimedEntryFunction` to _intentionally expose_ the entry function that the transaction is targeting to pre-decryption observers — required e.g. for Gas Station. The SDK sets this automatically for **sponsored or multisig** builds — you typically never set it manually. The one reason to override is when you want to expose only the module name and keep the function name hidden until decryption:

```typescript
options: {
  encrypted: true,
  feePayerAuthenticationKey: sponsor.publicKey.authKey(),
  claimedEntryFunction: {
    module: "0x1::aptos_account",
    // functionName intentionally omitted
  },
};
```

When you do set `functionName`, it must match the real entry function exactly.

## Caveats

- **Keyless, federated keyless, and account abstraction signers are not supported.** Encrypted transactions cannot be signed by these account types — the build/submit will throw before contacting the node.
- **Encrypted multisig is feature-gated** on some networks; submission may be rejected even when the SDK build succeeds. Watch your network's release notes.

## Further reading

- **[Encrypted Pending Transactions guide](/build/guides/encrypted-pending-transactions)** — product overview, visibility, AIP-144 spec, and cryptography paper.
- **[Aptos batch encryption (cryptography)](https://github.com/aptos-labs/aptos-core/tree/main/crates/aptos-batch-encryption)** — `aptos-core` implementation detail (optional reading).
